segments.s532-g17: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’; آن کرم را is a topical/object phrase with آن ‘that’ + کرم ‘the worm’ + را (definite object marker) marking the worm as the theme; گشت is the past of گشتن used here causatively/intransitively ‘became/turned’; صندوق تنگ is a nominal predicate ‘a narrow chest/box’ (صندوق = box, تنگ = narrow) — word order is Subject/Topic (آن کرم را) + verbal predicate (گشت) + predicate phrase (صندوق تنگ).
segments.s532-g18: یکی here functions as an indefinite/quantifier ‘one’ or ‘a certain’ introducing the action; حوض کردند is a compound verbal idea ‘they made/constructed a cistern’ (کردند = past plural ‘they made’); بر کوه سنگ is a prepositional phrase ‘upon the rock’ (بر = on, کوه سنگ = rock/mass of stone). Word order: (indefinite) + verb + location phrase.
segments.s532-g19: چو again = ‘when’; ساروج و سنگ lists two subjects connected by و ‘and’ (sاروج = mortar, سنگ = stone); از هوا گشت گرم is a causal/agentless phrase ‘became warm from the air’ where از هوا marks the source/reason. The verb گشت (became) governs the adjectival predicate گرم (warm).
segments.s532-g20: نهادند is the past plural of نهادن ‘they placed/set’; کرم is the direct object ‘the worm’; اندرو (an older form combining اندر + او) = ‘into it/within it’ indicating location; نرم نرم is a reduplicated adverbial expression ‘softly/gently’ (repetition intensifies manner). Word order is verb (نهادند) + object (کرم) + locative (اندرو) + manner (نرم نرم).
segments.s532-g21: چنان بد که is a fixed idiomatic sequence introducing a result clause (‘so much/so that’); بد here intensifies the degree rather than simply ‘evil’ — together they mean ‘so (much) that’; دارنده ‘the holder/keeper’ is the subject; هر بامداد ‘every morning’ is a habitual time adverbial. The conjunction که introduces the following clause.
segments.s532-g22: برفتی is an archaic/past narrative form meaning ‘(he) went/left’ (past habitual sense in narrative); دوان is an adverb/participle ‘running’ describing manner; از بر is a small locative construction here meaning ‘from beside/of’ and هفتواد is the proper name Haftvād. Word order: verb (برفتی) + manner (دوان) + from-phrase (از بر هفتواد).
segments.s532-g23: گزیدی is the past of گزیدن; here it means ‘gathered/selected/plucked’ (i.e. took fodder); به رنجش is an adverbial phrase ‘with toil/suffering’ (به + رنجش); علف ساختی is ‘you/he made/prepared grass/fodder’ where ساختی (past) means ‘made/arranged’. Overall this is a compact description: he laboriously gathered and prepared fodder.
segments.s532-g24: تن آگنده کرم آن is a noun phrase ‘the worm’s body, filled/full’ (تن = body, آگنده = filled/laden, کرم آن = that worm [genitive ordering is poetic]); پرداختی is a past-tense verb meaning broadly ‘gave/caused/paid’ (here used idiomatically ‘he supplied/caused it to become’ — i.e. he fattened/filled the worm). Word order is object phrase followed by the verb that reports the result.
segments.s532-g57: نتابید is a past-tense verb meaning 'did not endure / could not bear' (3rd singular) used impersonally here to indicate inability. Word order is inverted for poetry: the verb (نتابید) precedes the comitative phrase با او ('with him / against him'), the indefinite/negative noun کسی ('anyone' used in a negative sense = 'no one'), and the prepositional phrase بر به جنگ ('to/into battle'). Together they mean 'no one could bear to fight with him' or 'no one could stand against him in battle.'
segments.s532-g58: برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose / ensued.' The following small locution بر + ین (= برین) functions as a demonstrative adverb 'thereupon / on that,' with بر = 'upon' and ین as the archaic/poetic form of 'this/that.' نیز 'also' links this clause to the previous one. چندی درنگ is a noun phrase 'a short pause / a while's delay' (چندی = 'for a while'; درنگ = 'pause'). The whole segment means 'thereupon also a brief pause followed.'
segments.s532-g59: حصاری is a noun 'a fortress / fortified place.' شدش is the past-tense perfect of شدن with the enclitic -ش ('it/him'), here meaning 'became his' or 'came to be for him.' پر ز + noun is a poetic way to say 'full of X' (پر = 'full', ز = 'of/from'), so پر ز گنج و سپاه = 'full of treasure and army.' Word order places the resulting state after 'شدش.'
segments.s532-g60: ندیدی is past tense of 'دیدن' used impersonally/habitually here ('one did not see' / 'you would not see'). بران is a demonstrative adverb 'on that / thereon.' باره‌بر is a compound noun 'load-bearer / porter.' باد is literal 'wind' and is used poetically to describe the road (wind-swept); راه is 'road.' Together the phrase describes the road as exposed and empty: 'on that wind-swept road one would not see a porter.'
segments.s532-g49: چنان شد is a poetic, resumptive past: چنان (so/thus) + شد (became/was made) introduces the result. The noun phrase دژ نامور هفتواد follows as the complement ('the famed fortress Haftvād'); word order is inverted for poetic emphasis (verb before its complement).
segments.s532-g50: که introduces a subordinate/result clause ('so that/that'); گردش here means the fortress's circuit/rotation/turn (its walls or circuit). نیارست is an archaic/poetic negative or causative past form (meaning roughly 'did not allow / was not moved'), and جنبید باد is inverted word order (verb جنبید 'moved/stirred' before subject باد 'the wind'), so the clause says the wind could not make the circuit move.
segments.s532-g51: همی گشت is a habitual past/continuative ('kept becoming'); هر روز is an adverbial phrase 'every day'. برترش is comparative + enclitic 'ش' ('his/more elevated'), and بخت is the noun 'fortune/destiny' — together meaning 'his fortune became better each day' (adjective modifying the noun; the predicate is distributed over the whole clause).
segments.s532-g52: یکی here functions as a temporal adverb 'one [day] / once'; خویشتن را uses the emphatic/reflexive pronoun with the object marker را ('himself'). بیاراست is a past verb 'adorned' (bi‑arast), and سخت is an intensifying adverb 'firmly/strongly/richly' — together: 'one day he adorned himself greatly.'
segments.s532-g53: همی is the continuative marker 'continually/kept'; خواندندی is an archaic verbal form meaning 'they called/named' (poetic 3rd‑pl. formation), ورا is the indirect object 'to him', and شهریار is the predicate noun 'king/Şahriyar' — so 'they continually called him Shahriyar.'
segments.s532-g54: This clause is somewhat compressed by poetic word order: سر here is used as 'chief/leader' (سرِ), مرد بخرد = 'wise man' (مرد + adjectival بخرد 'wise'), ازو = 'from him / because of him', and در خمار = 'in intoxication / in a swoon/yearning'. Grammatically the phrase means 'the leader of the wise men was, because of him, in intoxication (smitten).' Note the ezāfe relationship is implied by word order rather than explicitly marked.
segments.s532-g55: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed / general' and که introduces a relative clause 'who'. بودی is an archaic past form 'was' (third‑person past), and به مرز اندرون places him 'at/on the inner border' (به + مکان + اندرون). The whole unit reads 'a spahbed who was at the inner frontier.'
segments.s532-g56: به یک چنگ is an instrumental phrase 'with one (single) cheng/strike' (چنگ used metaphorically as a single blow or grip), در جنگ 'in battle', کردش is the transitive past 'made/do him' (i.e., 'overcame/put him to shame'), and زبون is a predicative adjective 'abject/vanquished'. Word order places instrument/adverbials before the verb: 'with one stroke in battle he made him abase himself.'
segments.s532-g1: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before the place phrase. شهر کجاران is a place-expression (city/towns of Kajarān) with the noun before the verb; نفیر is the noun 'alarm/bugle-blast' and برآمد is the intransitive past verb 'arose/issued forth' taking نفیر as its subject.
segments.s532-g2: برفتند is the past plural form ('they went/they set out') with the prefixed/compound older form بر‑; با introduces instruments ('with'); the three weapon nouns are coordinated by و in a simple list.
segments.s532-g3: هیم here functions as a proper name (not the common noun 'fuel'), so none of the listed noun-senses fits exactly (marked -1). رفت is the simple past 'went'; پیش + اندرون = 'before/in the presence of / at the entrance of the inner quarters'; هفتواد is the proper name 'Haftvād' and is the location toward which he went.
segments.s532-g4: به marks direction/recipient ('to/into'); جنگ اندرون is a genitival/compositional phrase 'the fighting/war within' (جنگ = battle); داد here is the past verb 'gave/put' (i.e. 'sent/put into'); مردی 'a man' is the object; بداد is a noun meaning 'oppression' used here to state the consequence (oppression befell the man). The line uses compact classical word order where verbs and their objects can appear in terse sequence.
segments.s532-g5: همه شهر is a direct object phrase 'the whole city'; بگرفت is the past tense 'seized/took' with the prefixed verbal form ب‑ (classical style); و links clauses; او را is the marked definite object 'him' (را marks the object) and بکشت is an older past form 'he killed' (b‑ + کشت).
segments.s532-g6: بسی here functions adverbially to indicate large quantity (classical usage; the given sense list doesn't capture this exactly, so marked -1). گوهر و گنجش is a coordinated noun phrase 'gems and treasure'; آمد is the past 'came' and به مشت is a prepositional phrase 'into the hand(s)/into his grip' (مشت = handful/hand, used figuratively for 'possession').
segments.s532-g7: به نزدیک او is a locative phrase 'near him' (به + نزدیک = 'to/at near'); مردم انبوه شد literally 'people became numerous' — شد is the intransitive past 'became' used here to mean 'a crowd gathered' (classical usage where شدن marks a change of state).
segments.s532-g8: ز (poetic for از) marks origin 'from the towns'; کجاران is the plural/place-name 'the various towns/people of Kajarān'; سوی + کوه = 'toward the mountain'; شد again is a terse past verb meaning 'they went/moved/turned' (classical narrative uses شد flexibly for motion or change).
segments.s532-g9: Structure: an indefinite subject-marker 'یکی' introduces an act; 'دژ' (direct object) precedes the past verb 'بکرد' (he built/made) — a common older/poetic word order. The locative phrase 'از بر تیغ کوه' follows, where 'تیغ کوه' is a compound meaning 'the ridge (literally the blade) of the mountain' and 'از بر' here functions as a poetic locative 'upon/on'.
segments.s532-g10: The clause begins with the past verb 'شد' (became/happened) followed by the subject 'آن شهر' (that city) — inversion for emphasis. 'با او' means 'with him' (indicating alliance) and 'همه همگروه' ('all fellow-members') states the result: the whole city became his comrades/allied with him.
segments.s532-g11: 'نهاد' is the past of نهادن 'he placed/put'. 'اندران' = 'in/within it' (locative). The following words name what was placed: 'دژ' (the fortress) and 'دری آهنین' intends 'an iron door' (classical order: adjective after the noun). Note: the supplied lemma senses do not include 'door' for 'دری', so that specific sense must be marked absent.
segments.s532-g12: Parallel predication using هم ... هم ... ('both ... and ...'). The omitted subject (the fortress or what was placed) is understood; 'آرامگه' = 'resting-place/tomb' and 'جای کین' = 'place of vengeance'. 'بود' is the copula in past tense linking the two predicates.
segments.s532-g13: 'یکی' here introduces an indefinite existence ('a certain / there was a'), 'چشمه‌ای' is the indefinite noun 'a spring' (the -ای suffix), 'بود' = 'there was', and 'بر کوهسار' is the locative 'upon/on the mountainside'. Word order is subject + copula with the locative following.
segments.s532-g14: The phrase begins with the preposition 'ز' = 'from'. 'تخت' (here 'ledge/platform/throne') is the source; 'اندرآمد' is used verbally here as 'came forth/issued' (though the supplied senses gave only nominal meanings), and 'میان حصار' = 'in the midst of the enclosure/rampart'. Grammatically this is a source + verb + locative phrase describing where the spring issues from.
segments.s532-g15: 'یکی باره‌ای' is an adverbial phrase 'at one time/once' or 'on an occasion'; 'کرد' is the past 'he made/did'; 'گرداندرش' is a fused expression meaning 'a retinue around him' or 'troops about him' (he set/formed a guard). The whole phrase says he assembled/placed a company around himself.
segments.s532-g16: 'که' introduces the result/purpose clause. 'بینا' = 'visible/sighted'; 'به دیده' = 'to/by the eye' (instrumental); 'ندیدی' here functions as an impersonal/negative past 'was not seen / no one saw' (classical Persian allows such impersonal negative forms); 'سرش' = 'his head' (possessive enclitic 'ش'). The clause says that, as a result, his head was not seen by the eye.
segments.s532-g33: سپهبد (spahbed) is the head noun 'commander' followed by بدی, here best read as a postnominal adjective/epithet ('Badī' / 'evil' depending on context) modifying it. بر introduces a locative relation 'on/at', and دژ هفتواد is the compound 'the fortress of Haftvād' (هفتواد is a proper name). Word order Noun + epithet + preposition + place is normal in classical Persian.
segments.s532-g34: همان 'that/the very' points back to the preceding matter; پرسش کار is a nominal cluster meaning 'the issue/affair' (پرسش here functioning as 'matter/issue' with کار reinforcing it). The copula is omitted (typical in classical Persian), so the phrase should be taken as 'that same matter (was) [about] بیداد و داد' — بیداد و داد is a paired noun phrase 'injustice and justice'.
segments.s532-g35: A triad linked by و: سپاهی 'the soldiers/army', دستور 'the orders/command/administration', سالار 'the chiefs/commanders'. بار at the end functions as 'burden/charge/responsibility' associated with the preceding nouns, forming a compound idea 'the army, the command and the chiefs' charge'.
segments.s532-g36: هران 'those/every of them' + چیز 'things' makes 'those things'; کاید (from کید) means 'craft/plot/cunning', so چیزِ کاید = 'crafty things/measures'. شهان را uses را as a topic/emphasis marker ('as for the kings'), and به کار (بِه کار) here means 'into use / employed' — the clause is verbless/elliptical: 'all those crafty things (were) put to use by/for the kings' (classical Persian often omits an explicit auxiliary).
segments.s532-g37: همه 'all' + هرچ 'whatever/whatever thing' introduces a universal/indefinite object. بایستش is بایست (necessitative 'must / is necessary') with the enclitic -ش 'for him', so the phrase means 'all whatever was necessary for him'. آراستند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they adorned / they arranged', completing the clause.
segments.s532-g38: چنانچون 'just as / in the same way as' introduces the comparison. شهان را 'the kings' with را as object/topic marker, and بپیراستند (bi-pirāstand) is the past 3rd plural of آراستن with the prefix ب-; here it means 'they adorned' (i.e. 'they decked out / prepared for the kings'). The clause reads 'just as they adorned the kings.'
segments.s532-g39: به کشور 'into/through the country' is a locative phrase; پراگنده 'scattered' is an adjective/participle and شد (شد) is the past 3rd singular of شدن 'became' — together 'became scattered into the land'. لشکرش is 'his army' (لشکر + possessive suffix -ش) and functions as the logical subject/topic of the clause.
segments.s532-g40: همه 'all / the whole' + گشت (گشت) 'became' + آراسته 'adorned' forms the predicate 'all became adorned'. کشورش 'his country/realm' with the possessive suffix -ش appears at the end as the subject/topic; classical Persian permits this flexibility, so the line reads 'his whole country became adorned.'
segments.s532-g41: ز is the poetic preposition (classical form of از) marking origin: ز دریای چین = 'from the Sea of China.' دریای چین is a genitive phrase (دریا +ـِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِ of China). تا ... رسید = 'reached as far as ...'; تا + به + place (کرمان) gives the extent 'as far as Kerman.' رسید is simple past (3rd person) of رسیدن 'arrive/reach.'
segments.s532-g42: همه روی کشور is an adverbial phrase 'over the whole face/extent of the country' (همه modifies روی 'face/surface'). سپه is 'army/host' and گسترید (classical past) is the verb 'spread/extended' (3rd person past). Word order places the adverbial/object phrase before the verb, typical in poetic diction.
segments.s532-g43: پسر هفت is a poetic/orderly placing of the numeral after the noun meaning 'seven sons' (adjective/number follows noun in older/poetic usage). با introduces accompaniment 'with,' and تیغ‌زن is a compound agent noun 'sword-striker' = 'swordsman.' The phrase lists persons grouped with a following numeral phrase (ده هزار).
segments.s532-g44: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that very' linking to the preceding list; گنج is 'treasure.' با here marks accompaniment/association 'with,' and آلت کارزار is a genitive phrase 'the instruments/implements of war' (آلت = instrument/means, کارزار = battle/war). The whole segment names further possessions/equipment.
segments.s532-g45: هران is a distributive/classical 'each of those' (هر آن), followed by پادشا 'king.' Classical particle کو introduces a relative clause here ('which/whom'), and کشیدی is 2nd person singular past of کشیدن 'to draw/lead' so 'whom you drew/led into war.' The clause ends with the complement به جنگ 'into battle.'
segments.s532-g46: چو رفتی = 'when you went/when you departed' (چو = when; رفتی = 2nd singular past of رفتن). سپاهش is سپاه + possessive suffix ش = 'his army.' بر before a noun phrase plus an adjective (بر کرم تنگ) marks the circumstance/cause in classical usage; کرم تنگ = 'generosity narrow' = 'scarcity/shortness of generosity' (i.e. they were straitened by lack of generosity).
segments.s532-g47: شکسته شدی is a past passive/ resultative phrase 'you were broken/defeated' (شکسته = broken; شدی = 2nd singular past of شدن 'become/be (auxiliary)'). لشکری is an indefinite/nominal 'an army/host' and کامدی is an archaic past form 'came/arrived' (classical variant of آمد). The segment thus strings two short past events: defeat and the coming of a host.
segments.s532-g48: چو here means 'when.' آواز این داستان is a noun phrase 'the sound/rumor/tidings of this tale' (آواز used as 'report' or 'rumor' in epic style). بشندی is an archaic form whose usual meaning in this context is 'you heard' (classical شنویدن), but the supplied sense list does not include that sense, so its supplied senses do not fit exactly (see senses mapping). Grammatically the clause is temporal: 'when you heard the report of this story.'
segments.s532-g25: بر آمد is a past tense verbal phrase 'arose/rose up' (بر here functions as a directional/privative element fused with آمد). برین کار = 'that work/affair' (برین is an older/poetic form = بر + این, i.e. 'on/this/that'), and بر + پنج سال marks the duration 'for five years' (بر before a time-span = 'for/during' in older usage). Word order is somewhat compressed and poetic: verb + topic + duration.
segments.s532-g26: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. پیلی 'elephantine' is predicative; شد is the past tense 'became' (شد = became). آن کرم is the definite subject 'that grub/worm'. با + شاخ و یال = 'with horn and mane' describes its appearance. Note the compact, somewhat inverted poetic order: adjective/likeness (پیلی) appears immediately before شد.
segments.s532-g27: چو = 'when'. یک چند is an idiom meaning 'a little while / some time' (چند = 'a few / some'), and بگذشت is the past 'passed' (used impersonally for time passing). بر هفتواد here places the time/event at/over Haftvād (بر + toponym = 'at/in the domain of' in older/poetic usage).
segments.s532-g28: بر آواز = 'on/upon the voice/report of' (an idiom: 'at the news of'). آن کرم = 'that grub' modifies آواز. کرمان is the place/people 'Kerman' and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'put/laid/caused' used here in the sense 'set in motion / took action / rose' — an older, somewhat polyvalent verb. So the whole clause means that Kerman moved/acted upon hearing the report.
segments.s532-g29: همان = 'that same'; دخت = 'maiden/daughter'. خرم is an adjective 'joyful/pleasant' qualifying the daughter. نگهدار is a noun 'guardian/keeper' (appositive), and کرم names the thing kept ('the grub/worm') — so this is an appositive noun phrase: 'that same joyful maiden, the keeper of the grub.'
segments.s532-g30: پدر = 'father' (subject/topic). گشته is the perfect/participial form of شدن 'has become / became' (here translated as 'had become' / 'became'). جنگی is an adjectival qualifier 'warlike / military', سپهدار is the title 'sepahdār / commander'. کرم again is the genitive/possessive target ('of the grub' or 'connected to the grub'); the phrase says the father had become the warlike sepahdār (connected with) the grub — i.e. a martial commander related to that affair. Note poetic omission of linking particles.
segments.s532-g31: بیاراستندش is the past plural 'they arrayed/adorned him/provided for him' (bi- + آراستن in past). وزیر و دبیر = 'a vizier (minister) and a dabir (secretary/scribe)' — objects supplied to him. The clitic -ش on بیاراستندش = 'him/her'.
segments.s532-g32: برنجش is برنج + the enclitic -ش meaning 'his rice' or 'rice for him' (i.e. food at his table). خوردن here is the lexical verb 'to eat' (infinitive form used in narrative contexts); شهد و شیر = 'honey and milk' listed as foods. The word بدی in this line is lexically ambiguous in the provided sense list (the listed senses are 'evil / harm'), but in this context it functions idiomatically to indicate assorted/particular dishes or a qualitative remark about the food — i.e. the writer is listing what was eaten at his table. In short: 'he ate rice and various dishes, and honey and milk.'

sentences.s532-s9: When that worm's chest grew narrow, they made a cistern on the rock.
sentences.s532-s10: When the mortar and the stone grew warm from the air, they placed the worm inside it gently.
sentences.s532-s11: So (it was) that the keeper each morning would run from beside Haftvād.
sentences.s532-s12: He gathered fodder with hardship and thereby made that worm's body fat and full.
sentences.s532-s29: No one could stand against him in battle; thereupon a brief pause also followed.
sentences.s532-s30: A fortress became his, full of treasure and troops; on that wind-swept road one would not see a porter.
sentences.s532-s25: Thus was made the famed fortress of Haftvād, so firm that the wind could not stir its circuit.
sentences.s532-s26: Each day his fortune grew higher; one day he richly adorned himself.
sentences.s532-s27: They kept calling him 'Shahriyar', and the leader of the wise men, because of him, went into intoxication (was smitten).
sentences.s532-s28: There was a spahbed (general) who stood on the inner frontier; in battle he humbled him with a single stroke.
sentences.s532-s1: From the towns an alarm arose; they set off with spears, swords, and arrows.
sentences.s532-s2: Hīm went before Haftvād's inner court; he put a man into the inner fighting and oppression (fell on a man).
sentences.s532-s3: He took the whole city and killed him; many jewels and treasure came into his hands.
sentences.s532-s4: A crowd of people gathered near him; from the towns they moved toward the mountain.
sentences.s532-s5: He built a fortress on the mountain's ridge; that city, all together, became his companions.
sentences.s532-s6: He set in that fortress an iron door; it was both a resting-place and a place of vengeance.
sentences.s532-s7: There was a certain spring on the mountainside; it sprang forth from a ledge among the enclosure.
sentences.s532-s8: Once he set a troop/retinue about him, so that no eye could behold his head.
sentences.s532-s17: The spahbed Badī at the fortress of Haftvād — that very affair was one of injustice and justice.
sentences.s532-s18: The army, the command and the chiefs' charge — all those crafty measures the kings put to use in their affairs.
sentences.s532-s19: They arranged (provided) whatever was needed for him, just as they adorned the kings.
sentences.s532-s20: His army dispersed throughout the country; his whole realm became adorned.
sentences.s532-s21: His forces reached from the Sea of China as far as Kerman, and he spread the army over the whole face of the realm.
sentences.s532-s22: Seven sons, ten thousand swordsmen, and that same treasure with the implements of war.
sentences.s532-s23: Those kings whom you drew into battle—when you departed, his army was straitened by lack of generosity.
sentences.s532-s24: You were broken (defeated), and a host arrived when you heard the tidings of this tale.
sentences.s532-s13: That affair rose up and lasted for five years; when that grub became elephantine, with horn and mane.
sentences.s532-s14: When a short time had passed at Haftvād, at the news of that grub Kerman took action.
sentences.s532-s15: That same joyful maiden was the keeper of the grub; her father had become a warlike sepahdār of the grub.
sentences.s532-s16: They provided him with a vizier and a scribe; at his table he ate rice and various dishes, and honey and milk.
